question:
Let $1, 2, 3, \dots, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2009, 2012, 2016, \dots$
be a sequence defined by $x_k = k$ for $k=1, 2, \dots, 2006$ and
$x_{k+1} = x_k + x_{k-2005}$ for $k \geq 2006$. Show that the sequence has
2005 consecutive terms each divisible by 2006.
solution:
We first observe that given any sequence of integers
$x_1, x_2, \dots$ satisfying a recursion
\[
x_k = f(x_{k-1}, \dots, x_{k-n}) \qquad (k > n),
\]
where $n$ is fixed and $f$ is a fixed polynomial of $n$ variables with
integer coefficients, for any positive integer $N$, the sequence modulo $N$
is eventually periodic. This is simply because there are only finitely many
possible sequences of $n$ consecutive values modulo $N$, and once such
a sequence is repeated, every subsequent value is repeated as well.

We next observe that if one can rewrite the same recursion as
\[
x_{k-n} = g(x_{k-n+1}, \dots, x_k) \qquad (k > n),
\]
where $g$ is also a polynomial with integer coefficients, then
the sequence extends uniquely to a doubly infinite sequence $\dots,
x_{-1}, x_0, x_1, \dots$ which is fully periodic modulo any $N$.
That is the case in the
situation at hand, because we can rewrite the given recursion as
\[
x_{k-2005} = x_{k+1} - x_k.
\]
It thus suffices to find 2005 consecutive terms divisible by $N$ in the
doubly infinite sequence, for any fixed $N$ (so in particular for
$N = 2006$).
Running the recursion backwards, we easily find
\begin{gather*}
x_1 = x_0 = \cdots = x_{-2004} = 1 \\
x_{-2005} = \cdots = x_{-4009} = 0,
\end{gather*}
yielding the desired result.

descriptive_long.question: Let $1, 2, 3, \dots, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2009, 2012, 2016, \dots$ be a sequence defined by $termindex = indexk$ for $indexk=1, 2, \dots, 2006$ and $termnext = termindex + termoffset$ for $indexk \geq 2006$. Show that the sequence has 2005 consecutive terms each divisible by 2006.
descriptive_long.solution:
We first observe that given any sequence of integers termone, termtwo, $\dots$ satisfying a recursion\[ termindex = polyfuncf(x_{indexk-1}, \dots, termminusn) \qquad (indexk > spanlen), \]where $spanlen$ is fixed and $polyfuncf$ is a fixed polynomial of $spanlen$ variables with integer coefficients, for any positive integer $modulus$, the sequence modulo $modulus$ is eventually periodic. This is simply because there are only finitely many possible sequences of $spanlen$ consecutive values modulo $modulus$, and once such a sequence is repeated, every subsequent value is repeated as well.

We next observe that if one can rewrite the same recursion as\[ termminusn = polyfuncg(termminusnplus, \dots, termindex) \qquad (indexk > spanlen), \]where $polyfuncg$ is also a polynomial with integer coefficients, then the sequence extends uniquely to a doubly infinite sequence $\dots, termnegone, termzero, termone, \dots$ which is fully periodic modulo any $modulus$. That is the case in the situation at hand, because we can rewrite the given recursion as\[ termoffset = termnext - termindex. \]It thus suffices to find 2005 consecutive terms divisible by $modulus$ in the doubly infinite sequence, for any fixed $modulus$ (so in particular for $modulus = 2006$). Running the recursion backwards, we easily find\begin{gather*} termone = termzero = \cdots = termnegmmiv = 1 \\ termnegmmv = \cdots = termnegfourthousandnine = 0, \end{gather*}yielding the desired result.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: Let $1, 2, 3, \dots, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2009, 2012, 2016, \dots$ be a sequence defined by $sunflower = caterpillar$ for $caterpillar=1, 2, \dots, 2006$ and $blueberry = sunflower + raspberry$ for $caterpillar \geq 2006$. Show that the sequence has 2005 consecutive terms each divisible by 2006.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We first observe that given any sequence of integers $persimmon, pomegranate, \dots$ satisfying a recursion
\[
sunflower = nightshade(x_{caterpillar-1}, \dots, pineapple) \qquad (caterpillar > sandcastle),
\]
where $sandcastle$ is fixed and $nightshade$ is a fixed polynomial of $sandcastle$ variables with integer coefficients, for any positive integer $watermelon$, the sequence modulo $watermelon$ is eventually periodic. This is simply because there are only finitely many possible sequences of $sandcastle$ consecutive values modulo $watermelon$, and once such a sequence is repeated, every subsequent value is repeated as well.

We next observe that if one can rewrite the same recursion as
\[
pineapple = dragonfruit(blackberry, \dots, sunflower) \qquad (caterpillar > sandcastle),
\]
where $dragonfruit$ is also a polynomial with integer coefficients, then the sequence extends uniquely to a doubly infinite sequence $\dots, marigold, butternut, persimmon, \dots$ which is fully periodic modulo any $watermelon$. That is the case in the situation at hand, because we can rewrite the given recursion as
\[
raspberry = blueberry - sunflower.
\]
It thus suffices to find 2005 consecutive terms divisible by $watermelon$ in the doubly infinite sequence, for any fixed $watermelon$ (so in particular for $watermelon = 2006$). Running the recursion backwards, we easily find
\begin{gather*}
persimmon = butternut = \cdots = elderberry = 1 \\
gooseberry = \cdots = huckleberry = 0,
\end{gather*}
yielding the desired result.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $1, 2, 3, \dots, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2009, 2012, 2016, \dots$
be a sequence defined by $steadyelement = constant$ for $constant=1, 2, \dots, 2006$ and
$steadyelementnext = steadyelement + steadyelementshift$ for $constant \geq 2006$. Show that the sequence has
2005 consecutive terms each divisible by 2006.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We first observe that given any sequence of integers
$steadyelementone, steadyelementtwo, \dots$ satisfying a recursion
\[
steadyelement = constantfun(x_{constant-1}, \dots, steadyelementlag) \qquad (constant > variable),
\]
where $variable$ is fixed and $constantfun$ is a fixed polynomial of $variable$ variables with
integer coefficients, for any positive integer $infinite$, the sequence modulo $infinite$
is eventually periodic. This is simply because there are only finitely many
possible sequences of $variable$ consecutive values modulo $infinite$, and once such
a sequence is repeated, every subsequent value is repeated as well.

We next observe that if one can rewrite the same recursion as
\[
steadyelementlag = randomfun(steadyelementlagplus, \dots, steadyelement) \qquad (constant > variable),
\]
where $randomfun$ is also a polynomial with integer coefficients, then
the sequence extends uniquely to a doubly infinite sequence $\dots,
steadyelementnegone, steadyelementzero, steadyelementone, \dots$ which is fully periodic modulo any $infinite$.
That is the case in the
situation at hand, because we can rewrite the given recursion as
\[
steadyelementshift = steadyelementnext - steadyelement.
\]
It thus suffices to find 2005 consecutive terms divisible by $infinite$ in the
doubly infinite sequence, for any fixed $infinite$ (so in particular for
$infinite = 2006$).
Running the recursion backwards, we easily find
\begin{gather*}
steadyelementone = steadyelementzero = \cdots = steadyelementtwothousandfour = 1 \\
steadyelementtwothousandfive = \cdots = steadyelementfourthousandnine = 0,
\end{gather*}
yielding the desired result.

garbled_string.question:
Let $1, 2, 3, \dots, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2009, 2012, 2016, \dots$
be a sequence defined by $vodabuni = abeciroq$ for $abeciroq=1, 2, \dots, 2006$ and
$fujekaro = vodabuni + melicoro$ for $abeciroq \geq 2006$. Show that the sequence has
2005 consecutive terms each divisible by 2006.
garbled_string.solution:
We first observe that given any sequence of integers
jamodire, volematu, \dots satisfying a recursion
\[
vodabuni = qusareni(x_{abeciroq-1}, \dots, suvanepo) \qquad (abeciroq > geropazu),
\]
where $geropazu$ is fixed and $qusareni$ is a fixed polynomial of $geropazu$ variables with
integer coefficients, for any positive integer $hikotemu$, the sequence modulo $hikotemu$
is eventually periodic. This is simply because there are only finitely many
possible sequences of $geropazu$ consecutive values modulo $hikotemu$, and once such
a sequence is repeated, every subsequent value is repeated as well.

We next observe that if one can rewrite the same recursion as
\[
suvanepo = lopidwen(zomariti, \dots, vodabuni) \qquad (abeciroq > geropazu),
\]
where $lopidwen$ is also a polynomial with integer coefficients, then
the sequence extends uniquely to a doubly infinite sequence $\dots,
laduseni, nupatile, jamodire, \dots$ which is fully periodic modulo any $hikotemu$.
That is the case in the
situation at hand, because we can rewrite the given recursion as
\[
melicoro = fujekaro - vodabuni.
\]
It thus suffices to find 2005 consecutive terms divisible by $hikotemu$ in the
doubly infinite sequence, for any fixed $hikotemu$ (so in particular for
$hikotemu = 2006$).
Running the recursion backwards, we easily find
\begin{gather*}
jamodire = nupatile = \cdots = vekiraso = 1 \\
piburane = \cdots = xerulapi = 0,
\end{gather*}
yielding the desired result.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $(x_k)_{k\ge 1}$ be the integer sequence defined by
\[
  x_k=k\qquad (k=1,2,\dots ,2024),\qquad\qquad
  x_{k+1}=x_k+x_{k-2023}\qquad (k\ge 2024).
\]
Prove that the sequence contains $2023$ consecutive terms, each of which is divisible by $2024$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write $n=2023$ and $N=2024$.

1.  A finite-state description modulo $N$.
   ------------------------------------------------
   Work modulo $N$.  To evaluate the next term we need the block
   \((x_{k-n},x_{k-n+1},\dots ,x_k)\).  Indeed the recursion gives
   \[
        x_{k+1}\equiv x_k+x_{k-n}\pmod N.
   \]
   Conversely the same formula can be solved for the oldest entry:
   \[
        x_{k-n}\equiv x_{k+1}-x_k\pmod N.
   \]
   Hence the $(n+1)$-tuple
   \[
       \mathbf v_k=(x_{k-n},x_{k-n+1},\dots ,x_k)\pmod N
   \]
   determines \emph{both} its successor $\mathbf v_{k+1}$ and its predecessor
   $\mathbf v_{k-1}$.  Reducing modulo $N$ there are only $N^{n+1}$ possible
   $(n+1)$-tuples, so the map $\mathbf v_k\mapsto\mathbf v_{k+1}$ is a
   permutation of a finite set.  Therefore the orbit of any initial
   $(n+1)$-tuple is purely periodic; i.e. there exists a positive integer $T$
   such that
   \[
        x_{k+T}\equiv x_k\pmod N\qquad\text{for every integer }k. \tag{1}
   \]
   In particular the doubly-infinite sequence
   $$(\dots ,x_{-1},x_0,x_1,\dots)$$
   that we shall construct will be $T$-periodic modulo $N$.

2.  Extending the sequence backwards.
   ----------------------------------
   Besides $x_1,\dots ,x_n$ we are also given $x_{n+1}=N=2024$.  Knowing the
   $(n+1)$ consecutive values $(x_1,\dots ,x_{n+1})$ allows us to recover the
   whole sequence inductively to the left: for $k\ge n+1$,
   \[x_{k-n}=x_{k+1}-x_k.\]
   Thus one obtains uniquely defined integers $x_0,x_{-1},\dots$.

3.  An explicit backward calculation.
   ----------------------------------
   Starting with
   \[x_{n+1}=2024,\qquad x_n=2023,\qquad x_{n-1}=2022,\;\dots ,\;x_1=1,\]
   apply $x_{k-n}=x_{k+1}-x_k$ successively:
   \[
      \begin{aligned}
          x_0 &= x_{n+1}-x_n       = 2024-2023 = 1,\\
          x_{-1} &= x_n-x_{n-1}        = 2023-2022 = 1,\\
          &\,\vdots\\
          x_{-(n-1)} &= x_2-x_1        = 2-1 = 1,\\[2mm]
          x_{-n} &= x_1-x_0          = 1-1 = 0.
      \end{aligned}
   \]
   Continuing the same way,
   \[
         x_{-n-1}=x_0-x_{-1}=0,\;x_{-n-2}=x_{-1}-x_{-2}=0,\;\dots ,\;x_{-2n+1}=0.
   \]
   Hence
   \[
        x_{-n},x_{-n-1},\dots ,x_{-2n+1}
   \]
   are $n=2023$ consecutive zeros, and therefore each is divisible by $N$.

4.  Transferring the zero block to positive indices.
   -------------------------------------------------
   Let $T$ be the period provided by (1).  Pick an integer $m$ large enough so
   that
   \[-2n+1+mT\;\ge\;1.\]
   Shifting the previously found block forward by $mT$ indices and using
   (1) yields
   \[x_{-2n+1+mT}=x_{-2n+1}\equiv 0\pmod N,\quad\dots ,\quad
     x_{-n+mT}=x_{-n}\equiv 0\pmod N.\]
   All these indices are now $\ge1$, so they belong to the original
   one-sided sequence $(x_k)_{k\ge1}$.  Thus the sequence possesses $n=2023$
   consecutive terms that are multiples of $2024$, as required.

core_steps: Finite-state/Pigeonhole: a fixed-order polynomial recursion is eventually periodic modulo any N.
Invertibility: because x_{k-n} can be written as x_{k+1}-x_k, the sequence extends uniquely backward, so it becomes doubly-infinite and fully periodic mod N.
Backward computation from the given seed (x_k=k for k=1,…,n+1) produces n consecutive zero terms; hence those n terms are multiples of N.

slot1.description: Order n of the recursion (gap between indices, also number of consecutive divisible terms to be found).

slot2.description: Modulus N for which divisibility is required.
